1: The Great Oracle Against Babylon occupies two full chapters (50-51) — the longest single oracle in the book and arguably the most theologically dense. The superscription: hadavar asher diber YHWH el-Bavel el-eretz Kasdim beyad Yirmiyahu hanavi. The oracle is addressed to Babylon as a whole and to the land of the Chaldeans specifically — the Chaldeans being the ruling Babylonian dynasty (Neo-Babylonian dynasty founded by Nabopolassar in 626 BCE). The prophetic medium is specified: beyad Yirmiyahu (through/by the hand of Jeremiah) — the hand-formula for prophetic transmission emphasizes that this is mediated divine speech.
2: The proclamation formula to the nations: hagidu vagoyim vehashmi-u (announce and proclaim). Raise the signal banner, proclaim without hiding. What follows is the core announcement: Babylon is taken, Bel is put to shame, Marduk is dismayed, her images are put to shame, her idols are dismayed. Bel (title meaning 'lord') was the title of Marduk, the supreme god of Babylon — the Babylonian national deity who was believed to have given Nebuchadnezzar his victories. Chofer and chatat (shamed and dismayed) — the terms for divine defeat. When Babylon falls, Marduk's power is refuted. The proclamation to the nations inverts the Babylonian victory announcements that went out when Babylon conquered others.
3: A nation from the north (goy mitsafon) will come against Babylon and make her land a desolation. The 'foe from the north' that Jeremiah repeatedly applied to Babylon itself (Jeremiah 1:14; Jeremiah 4:6; Jeremiah 6:22) is now turned against Babylon. The instrument of judgment becomes the object of judgment. Historically, this was fulfilled by Cyrus the Great of Persia (Achaemenid dynasty) who conquered Babylon in 539 BCE — the Persians came from the north and east. The formula 'man nor beast will dwell there' (ein yoshev bahem) echoes the desolation pattern applied to Judah in Jeremiah 4:25; now Babylon receives the same.
4: Bayamim hahem uveet hahi neum YHWH yavo-u venei Yisrael hemmah uvnei Yehudah yachdav: 'In those days and at that time, declares the LORD, the people of Israel and the people of Judah will come together.' The joint return of northern and southern kingdoms is a recurring restoration promise (Jeremiah 3:18; Jeremiah 31:6, 31-34; Jeremiah 33:7). They will weep as they come (haloch vivecheh yelekhu) — penitential tears accompanying the return; and seek YHWH their God. The covenant-seeking formula: the return from exile is not merely geographic but theological — it is the people turning to their God (cf. Deuteronomy 30:1-6 where the return from exile is contingent on return to YHWH).
5: Tziyon yish-alu derekh hennah peneihem yilekhu: They will ask the way to Zion with their faces turned toward it. This is the reversal of exile: exile is face-turned-away from Zion (the covenantal geography of presence); return is face-turned-toward Zion. The covenant renewal they seek is described as berit olam (eternal/everlasting covenant) — the phrase that characterizes the new covenant of Jeremiah 31:31-34 and the Davidic covenant of Jeremiah 33:20-21. Lo tishakhach (not to be forgotten) — this covenant is permanent; no future apostasy will annul it. The return from Babylon is the beginning of the fulfillment of the new covenant promise.
6: Tzon ovedet hayu ammi: 'My people have been like lost sheep.' The shepherd-and-sheep covenant image runs through Jeremiah: the false shepherds of Jeremiah 23 who led the flock astray; the promise of a good shepherd in Jeremiah 23:4-5. Here, the sheep 'wandered from mountain to hill' — the high-place worship that characterized Israel's apostasy; they 'forgot their resting place' (mavtzehem) — the covenantal resting place of YHWH's presence. Their enemies devoured them without guilt, 'because they sinned against the LORD, the true habitation of righteousness, the LORD, the hope of their fathers' (Jeremiah 50:7). The guilt-logic: the nations attacked Israel without their own guilt because Israel's sin invited the attack.
7: The enemy's self-exculpation: lo ne-esham (we are not guilty) — because Israel sinned against YHWH. This is the logic of covenant curse: when YHWH's people are punished, the nations who execute the judgment can claim they acted under divine sanction. But this logic has limits — the instruments of judgment can themselves be judged for excess or pride (cf. Isaiah 10:5-15 where Assyria is punished for its arrogance in executing YHWH's judgment). YHWH, the neveh tzedeq (habitation of righteousness) and mikveh avoteihem (hope of their fathers) — two divine epithets: the place where justice dwells and the object of ancestral hope.
8: The exodus command: nud mitorev Bavel (flee from the midst of Babylon). Isaiah 48:20 gives the same command: 'Come out of Babylon, flee from the Chaldeans.' Revelation 18:4 repeats it for the eschatological Babylon: 'Come out of her, my people.' The exodus pattern: YHWH's people are to leave the place of captivity and return to the covenant land — just as the Exodus from Egypt structured the first redemption. The he-goat (ka-ayelim lifnei tzon) image: the lead animal at the front of the flock. The exiles are to take initiative and lead the way out.
9: An assembly of great nations from the north stirred up against Babylon: the Medo-Persian alliance that Cyrus led was indeed a coalition of peoples. Gedudim (companies/troops) arranged against Babylon. The arrows of the coalition will not miss: lo tashov reyqam (they will not return empty) — a warrior's arrows hitting their mark. The cosmic precision of divine judgment: the arrows of YHWH's appointed instrument are guided to their target.
10: Chaldea as plunder: vehayetah Kasdim leshallal kol sholaleiha yisba-u (Chaldea will become plunder; all who plunder her will be satisfied). The Babylonians who plundered the nations will themselves be plundered — the retributive mirror of covenant justice. The Cyrus Cylinder (539 BCE) describes Cyrus restoring temple treasures to their places: the plunder that Babylon took from the nations was redistributed when Babylon fell. Ezra 1:7-11 records Cyrus returning the temple vessels Nebuchadnezzar had taken from Jerusalem.
11: The indictment of Babylon's joy: they 'rejoiced and exulted' (samachtem ve-aloztem) as plunderers of YHWH's inheritance. The heifer-on-grass (ka-eglah doresheh) image depicts unrestrained, carefree joy — the animal sporting in a meadow, free of burden. This unrestrained celebration at Judah's destruction inverts what Babylon should have felt: any serious engagement with what they had done — destroying YHWH's city and temple, deporting his people — should have produced sobriety, not exuberance. The neighing (tzahaltun ka-abirim, neighed like stallions) adds the image of full-throated, uncontained pride.
12: Boshah imchem meod chafrah yoladtechem: 'Your mother will be utterly ashamed; the one who bore you will be disgraced.' Babylon's 'mother' — the Chaldean dynasty (Nabopolassar, the founder) or the city-goddess — will be put to shame. The end of the verse: acharit goyim midbar tziyah va-aravah (she will become the last of the nations — a wilderness, a dry land, a desert). Three progressively barren terms: wilderness, dry, desert. Babylon's lush Mesopotamian territory, irrigated by the Euphrates and Tigris, will become like the desert wastelands. Archaeological and geographic surveys confirm that after Alexander's death and the shift of population to Seleucia, the ancient city of Babylon progressively abandoned and was eventually completely deserted.
13: Because of YHWH's wrath (miqetzef YHWH), she will not be inhabited — shemamah kula (completely desolate). The hissing passersby (yishreq) echo the covenant curse formula: Babylon receives the same response from passersby that Jerusalem received (Jeremiah 18:16; Jeremiah 19:8). The irony is complete: the nation that gloated over Jerusalem's fall becomes itself the object of travelers' horrified hissing.
14: The battle deployment against Babylon: erekhu al-Bavel misaviv (deploy yourselves against Babylon from every side). All who draw the bow — the Persian armies were famous archers (Herodotus describes the Persian-Median archers in detail). The command: 'do not spare your arrows' (al tachmosu al chetz). The reason: ki le-YHWH chata-ah (for she has sinned against the LORD). Babylon's sin is the basis of the military campaign: this is not merely geopolitical conquest but covenant-consequential judgment.
15: Shout against Babylon from every side — she has surrendered (nattanah yadah, she has given her hand). The surrendered hand is the ANE gesture of submission: a defeated king extended his hand to the victor to receive a cord or chain. Her foundations have fallen (naflu ashuteha): not merely the walls but the deep foundations of Babylonian civilization. Ki niqmat YHWH hi: this is the vengeance of the LORD — the retributive justice that returns on Babylon what it did to others. 'As she has done, do to her' is the lex talionis of covenant justice applied to nations.
16: Cut off the sower and the reaper: the agricultural cycle of Babylon — planting and harvest — is disrupted. The foreign workers flee: mipnei cherev hayonah ish el-ammo yifnu (before the sword of the oppressor, everyone will return to their own people). The foreign labor force — migrants and subjugated peoples working Babylonian fields — disperses at the first sign of military collapse. This was historically accurate: Babylon's agricultural economy depended on subject peoples; their departure at the Persian conquest accelerated the city's economic decline.
17: Seh pezurah Yisrael arayot hiddichu: 'Israel is a scattered sheep; lions have driven it away.' The scattered-sheep image (Jeremiah 50:6) returns. Two sequential predators are named: first the king of Assyria devoured it, last Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon has gnawed his bones. The two-stage destruction of Israel: the Assyrian conquest of the north (722 BCE) and the Babylonian conquest of the south (586 BCE). Both are identified as lions — powerful predators — but both will be punished in turn (Jeremiah 50:18). The chronological sequence (first Assyria, then Babylon) structures Judah's history within the oracle.
18: Laken koh amar YHWH Tzvaot elohe Yisrael: therefore YHWH will punish the king of Babylon and his land just as he punished the king of Assyria (ka-asher paqadti al melech Ashur). Assyria's fate is the precedent for Babylon's. Assyria was destroyed by the Babylonian-Median alliance in 614-612 BCE (fall of Nineveh) and 610-609 BCE (fall of Harran) — within Jeremiah's lifetime. The same God who brought down Assyria will bring down Babylon. The historical precedent validates the prophetic claim.
19: The restoration of Israel to its pasture: vehashiboti et-Yisrael el-navehu (I will bring Israel back to his pasture). The geographic specificity — Carmel, Bashan, Ephraim, Gilead — represents all of Israel's most productive territories. Carmel (fertile coastal range), Bashan (rich tableland east of the Sea of Galilee, famous for cattle — Amos 4:1), Ephraim (the central highlands), Gilead (Transjordanian highlands). Tishba nafsho (his appetite/soul will be satisfied) — the soul-satisfaction formula of Psalms 107:9 ('he satisfies the thirsty soul and fills the hungry soul with good things'). Return from exile means complete restoration of the covenant life.
20: Bayamim hahem uveet hahi neum YHWH yevuqqash et-avon Yisrael veeynenu: 'In those days and at that time, declares the LORD, Israel's iniquity will be searched for, but there will be none, and the sins of Judah will not be found; for I will forgive those I spare.' This is the most comprehensive forgiveness formula in Jeremiah — total iniquity-erasure. The connection to Jeremiah 31:34 ('I will forgive their iniquity and remember their sin no more') is explicit: the new covenant promise of complete forgiveness, applied to the restoration from Babylon as its initial fulfillment. The search for iniquity yields nothing — it has been expunged. NT application: 1 John 1:9 ('he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness') and Hebrews 10:17-18 ('their sins and their lawless acts I will remember no more').
21: Merathaim and Pekod: two geographic locations with Hebrew wordplay. Merathaim (double rebellion/bitterness) is a wordplay on Marratu, the Babylonian name for the marshy area at the confluence of the Tigris and Euphrates (the Persian Gulf region). Pekod (visitation/punishment) is a wordplay on Puqudu, an Aramean tribe in southern Babylonia. The puns make the oracle's meaning visible in the geography: YHWH has built judgment into the very names of Babylon's territories. Cherem (complete destruction/ban): the same term used for holy war in Joshua — the Canaanites were devoted to cherem destruction; now Babylon receives the same designation.
22: Qol milchamah ba-aretz veshever gadol: the sound of battle and great destruction. The two terms — sound of battle (qol milchamah) and great destruction (shever gadol) — are the two dimensions of the fall: the audible chaos of combat and the visible wreckage left behind. The compressed brevity of the verse (five Hebrew words) is itself expressive: the disaster arrives with blunt, sudden force.
23: Eikh niqqatz veyishaver pattish kol-ha-aretz: 'How the hammer of the whole earth has been cut to pieces and broken!' Babylon as the hammer of the whole earth (pattish kol-ha-aretz) — YHWH's instrument for striking the nations (cf. Jeremiah 51:20-23 where Babylon is 'my war club' and 'weapon of war'). The hammer is now broken: the instrument that was used to beat others is itself beaten. The exclamation eikh (how!) is the lament particle of Lamentations 1:1 ('How lonely sits the city'). Even Babylon's fall elicits something like lament at the magnitude of what has happened.
24: Yaqoshti lach vegam nillakhat Bavel: 'I laid a snare for you and you were caught, O Babylon, before you knew it.' The divine hunter who sets snares (cf. 5:26 where the wicked of Israel set snares for men; now YHWH sets the snare for Babylon). The surprise of capture — lo yadata (before you knew it) — reflects the historical suddenness of Babylon's fall: according to Herodotus and the Nabonidus Chronicle, Cyrus entered Babylon in 539 BCE while Belshazzar held a feast (Dan 5). The reason: ki el-YHWH higgarat (because you strove against the LORD). Striving against YHWH is the fundamental category of Babylonian sin — not merely political overreach but ontological hubris.
25: YHWH has opened his armory (pithach YHWH et-otzaro) and brought out the weapons of his anger (kele za-amo). The divine armory is the arsenal of cosmic warfare — the divine warrior's weapons. The work of the Lord GOD of hosts is in the land of the Chaldeans — the phrase melechet Adonai YHWH Tzvaot (the work of the Lord GOD of hosts) frames Babylon's destruction as a divine project, not a human military achievement. Cyrus is the instrument; YHWH is the agent.
26: The siege commands: attack from every end (miqetz ba-u lah), open her granaries (pitchu mizbanoieha), pile her up like heaps (soluha ka-aremim), completely destroy her, let nothing remain. The granary-opening image: Babylon's immense grain stores (Herodotus describes Babylon's twenty-year food supply) are opened for plunder. The heap-image (aremim): grain heaps, but also ruin-mounds — tel. Both meanings converge: the grain stores become ruins.
27: Slaughter all her bulls — her military leadership, the most powerful men. The bovine metaphor for warriors (cf. Psalms 22:12: 'strong bulls of Bashan surround me') is standard ANE imagery. The killing ground (yedu latebach, go down to the slaughter) echoes the sacrifice-slaughter image. Oi lahem ki va yomam eit piqudatam: Woe to them, for their day has come — the time of their reckoning. Eit piqudat is the appointed-time-of-visitation formula: the date for covenant reckoning was set in YHWH's calendar before Babylon ever conquered Judah.
28: Those who flee from Babylon declare YHWH's vengeance in Zion: mesha-eim uferiteimim me-eretz Bavel lehagid beTziyon et-niqmat YHWH. The fugitives' proclamation of YHWH's vengeance in Zion serves an apologetic purpose: the destruction of Babylon is declared to be specifically about the temple. Niqmat heichalo (vengeance for his temple): the burning of the temple in 586 BCE is the specific grievance; Babylon's fall is YHWH vindicating his own house. The theme of temple-vengeance grounds the geopolitical event in covenant theology.
29: Archers summoned against Babylon: encamp all around, let none escape, give her according to her work (shalem la kema-aseiha), act to her according to all she has done (kekol asher-asatah asu lah). The proportionality principle: Babylon receives exactly what she gave. The specific provocation: ki el-YHWH zadah (for she has acted arrogantly against the LORD), against the Holy One of Israel. The title qadosh Yisrael (Holy One of Israel) is characteristic of Isaiah (used 26 times in Isa 1-66) but appears here in Jeremiah — the rarity marks the intensity of the theological claim: Babylon has offended YHWH's holiness itself.
30: Young men fallen in the streets, warriors silenced: the standard formula of city-fall applied to Babylon (cf. Lamentations 2:21; Jeremiah 49:26; Jeremiah 50:30). The repetition across the oracles against multiple nations establishes a pattern: YHWH's judgment formula is consistently applied regardless of which nation receives it. The universality of the judgment pattern — no city exempt — is itself the theological point.
31: Hinneni eleicha zadon: 'Behold, I am against you, O most arrogant.' Zadon (arrogance/pride) is personified as Babylon itself — the empire is its arrogance. The divine adversative (hinneni eleicha) appears throughout Jeremiah as the covenant lawsuit formula for divine opposition. The time has come: ki va yomcha eit piqadticha (your day has come, the time I will punish you). YHWH appointed the time of Babylon's punishment before it happened — the oracle was given decades before Cyrus.
32: The most arrogant will stumble and fall (ve-kashel ve-nafal hazadon) — no one to raise him up. The sequence of stumbling and falling is the same vocabulary used for Israel's fall in Amos 5:2 ('fallen, no more to rise, is the virgin Israel'). The same fate now comes to the empire that caused Israel to fall. Fire set to Babylon's cities consuming all around: the burning of cities is the standard conquest-conclusion in the ancient Near East (the Lachish reliefs of Sennacherib show Assyrian fires consuming Judean cities).
33: The shared oppression: koh amar YHWH Tzvaot ashukot benei Yisrael uvnei Yehudah yachdav (the people of Israel are oppressed, and the people of Judah with them). All their captors hold them fast (kol shoveichem hecheziku vam) and refuse to release them (me-anu shalacham). The legal-rescue framing: YHWH as the kinsman-redeemer (go-el) who pleads the cause of the held-captive. The captors' refusal to release triggers the goel's action: when human captors will not release, the divine redeemer acts directly.
34: Go-alam chazaq YHWH Tzvaot shemo riv yariv et-rivam: 'Their Redeemer is mighty; the LORD of hosts is his name. He will certainly plead their cause.' The go-el (kinsman-redeemer) title for YHWH: in Israelite law, the go-el was the nearest male relative who had the right and obligation to redeem a family member from slavery, avenge his blood, or redeem mortgaged family land. YHWH is the ultimate go-el (cf. Isaiah 43:14; Jeremiah 44:6, 24; Jeremiah 47:4; 48:17 where 'go-el' is a major Isaiah title for YHWH). The strongest kinsman who owes redemption-loyalty will act — Babylon has enslaved what belongs to him.
35: The sword litany begins: cherev al-Kasdim (a sword against the Chaldeans). The sequence of sword-against in Jeremiah 50:35-38 is a poem — seven recipients of the sword: inhabitants of Babylon (Jeremiah 50:35), officials and wise men (Jeremiah 50:35), false prophets (Jeremiah 50:36), warriors (Jeremiah 50:36), horses and chariots (Jeremiah 50:37), foreign troops (Jeremiah 50:37), treasures (Jeremiah 50:37), waters (Jeremiah 50:38). The comprehensiveness maps every component of Babylonian power. The prophets and wise men (chakhameha) — Babylon's divination establishment (the baru priests, astrologers, omen-readers) were famous throughout the ancient world; Daniel 1:20 notes the Babylonian wise men's status at court. Their sword-judgment means Babylon's intelligence apparatus will fail.
36: A sword against her false prophets (badim, diviners/liars) — they will become fools (venoallu). The Hebrew badim refers specifically to false prophets or diviners; the root suggests those who speak empty or false words. Babylon's religious-political prophecy establishment — which consistently predicted Babylonian invincibility — will be exposed as foolish when the empire falls. A sword against her warriors, so they become dismayed: the military elite who formed Babylon's professional army (the gabbe soldiers) will lose heart.
37: A sword against her horses and chariots: the chariot corps was Babylon's mechanized warfare. A sword against the foreign troops (ha-erev): the mixed multitude of mercenaries and subject-peoples in Babylon's army. They will become like women (vehaytem lanashim) — the idiom for complete loss of martial courage (cf. Isaiah 19:16; Nahum 3:13). A sword against her treasures: the immense wealth of Babylon — plundered from nations, hoarded in temples and palaces — becomes subject to plunder itself.
38: A drought upon her waters: chorev al-meiha veyvoshu (drought upon her waters and they will dry up). This is wordplay: cherev (sword) in Jeremiah 50:35-37 shifts to chorev (drought/heat) in Jeremiah 50:38 — the two words differ only in vocalization. The Euphrates and its canals were the lifeblood of Babylon; Cyrus's strategy according to Herodotus involved diverting the Euphrates to allow his troops to enter through the dry riverbed. The drying of the waters is both literal (the military tactic) and theological (YHWH withdrawing the life-support of Babylon). Ki eretz pesilim hi (for it is a land of idols) — the worship of images (pesilim) is the spiritual root of Babylon's judgment.
39: Wild desert creatures and jackals will inhabit Babylon; ostriches will live there. The three desolation-animals (demons of the wilderness, jackals, ostriches) appear in the similar desolation oracles of Isaiah 13:21-22 and Jeremiah 34:13-15 — standard ancient Near Eastern imagery for the complete withdrawal of human civilization and its replacement by the creatures of uninhabited waste. Lo-teshev la-netzach (she shall never again be inhabited) — the permanent desolation formula. Archaeological evidence: after Alexander the Great's death, plans to rebuild Babylon were abandoned; the city was progressively deserted as population moved to Seleucia on the Tigris. By the first century CE, Babylon was a ruin inhabited by Parthian soldiers.
40: The Sodom-Gomorrah comparison applied to Babylon: the same comparison made for Edom (Jeremiah 49:18) now describes Babylon. Both Edom and Babylon receive the ultimate judgment-type — complete, permanent, uninhabitable. The repetition reinforces that Jeremiah applies the same theological framework to all the nations: YHWH's judgment on pagan powers uses the Sodom-Gomorrah template as its benchmark. Lo yeshev sham ish (no man will dwell there): the formula of absolute desolation that applies to both individuals and communities.
41: A people coming from the north — a great nation, many kings roused from the ends of the earth. The Medo-Persian coalition: Cyrus united the Medes (who had destroyed Nineveh in 612 BCE), the Persians, and allied peoples from across the Iranian plateau. Yaqitzu (roused): the awakening-of-kings image suggests divine mobilization — YHWH rousing the nations as his instruments, as he roused Nebuchadnezzar against Judah (Jeremiah 1:15; Jeremiah 5:15-17).
42: They hold bow and spear; cruel, showing no mercy. Their roaring like the sea (yish-agu kayam): the coalition army's voice fills the land like the surf — overwhelming, constant, irresistible. On horses, arrayed like a man for battle against Babylon. The Persian-Median horse cavalry was formidable; the royal Persian Immortals (10,000 elite troops) were the most professional army of their era. The same phrase that Jeremiah used to describe the Babylonian threat against Judah (Jeremiah 6:23: 'they are cruel and show no mercy; their voice roars like the sea') is now applied to the threat against Babylon.
43: The king of Babylon hears the report and his hands go limp: the same reaction that Zedekiah and the kings of Judah had when they heard of Babylon's advance. The reversal is exact and deliberate: the empire that caused Judah's kings to tremble will itself tremble. Chil kayoleda (anguish like a woman in labor): the universal ANE image for paralytic fear. Nebuchadnezzar — or his successors — the great conqueror, reduced to childbirth-pain helplessness.
44: The lion-from-Jordan image that was used for YHWH against Edom (Jeremiah 49:19) now appears against Babylon. The repetition of the image frames the OAN section as a unified whole: YHWH uses the same hunting strategy against all the nations. The rhetorical question — who is appointed over Babylon? — has a clear answer: YHWH himself. He will appoint his chosen one over her. Historically, YHWH's chosen one for this task was Cyrus — explicitly named in Isaiah 44:28 and 45:1 as YHWH's anointed and his shepherd.
45: Lachen shim-u etzat YHWH asher ya-atz el-Bavel umachshevotav asher chashav el-eretz Kasdim: hear the strategy of YHWH against Babylon and his purposes against the Chaldeans. The young of the flock (tz-irei hatzon) will be dragged away — the same phrase used for Edom (Jeremiah 49:20). The pasture-horror: asham aleihem naveihem (their pasture will be appalled at them). YHWH's plan is fixed and announced ahead of time: prophecy is the advance announcement of history from the divine council.
46: At the sound of Babylon's capture the earth will quake (tera-ash ha-aretz); the cry will be heard among the nations (yishama bagoyim za-aqatah). The cosmic scale of Babylon's fall: the earth itself shakes. The Babylonian empire was the dominant power for seventy years — its fall in 539 BCE was a seismic geopolitical event whose reverberations were felt throughout the ancient Near East. The cry heard among the nations is the inverse of Jeremiah 50:2's proclamation: the announcement goes out to the nations that the empire which had dominated them is ended. Revelation 18:9-10, 18-19 uses these images for the eschatological fall of Babylon: kings of the earth weeping, crying 'woe, woe for the great city' when they see her smoke rising.
